sentence: { "2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.3 Variable header -> 2.3.1 Packet Identifier -> Table 2.5 - Control Packets that contain a Packet Identifier": [ { "Original": "**Control Packet** | **Packet Identifier field** ---|--- CONNECT | NO CONNACK | NO PUBLISH | YES (If QoS > 0) PUBACK | YES PUBREC | YES PUBREL | YES PUBCOMP | YES SUBSCRIBE | YES SUBACK | YES UNSUBSCRIBE | YES UNSUBACK | YES PINGREQ | NO PINGRESP | NO DISCONNECT | NO The Client and Server assign Packet Identifiers independently of each other.", "Adjusted": "The Client and Server assign Packet Identifiers independently of each other." }, { "Original": "As a result, Client Server pairs can participate in concurrent message exchanges using the same Packet Identifiers.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "**Non normative comment** It is possible for a Client to send a PUBLISH Packet with Packet Identifier 0x1234 and then receive a different PUBLISH with Packet Identifier 0x1234 from its Server before it receives a PUBACK for the PUBLISH that it sent.", "Adjusted": "It is possible for a Client to send a PUBLISH Packet with Packet Identifier 0x1234 and then receive a different PUBLISH Packet with Packet Identifier 0x1234 from its Server before the Client receives a PUBACK for the PUBLISH Packet that it sent." }, { "Original": "Client Server PUBLISH Packet Identifier=0x1234---Ã Ã\x9f\\--PUBLISH Packet Identifier=0x1234 PUBACK Packet Identifier=0x1234---Ã Ã\x9f\\--PUBACK Packet Identifier=0x1234", "Adjusted": "A Client sends a PUBLISH Packet with Packet Identifier 0x1234, and a Server sends a different PUBLISH Packet with Packet Identifier 0x1234.
enhanced_sentence: In the MQTT protocol, the Client and Server assign Packet Identifiers independently of each other. Consequently, a Client-Server pair can engage in concurrent message exchanges using identical Packet Identifier values. For example, a Client may send a PUBLISH Packet with Packet Identifier 0x1234 and subsequently receive a different PUBLISH Packet, also with Packet Identifier 0x1234, from the Server before the Client receives a PUBACK for its original PUBLISH Packet. This is possible because the Packet Identifier field is present in specific control packets: PUBLISH (when QoS is greater than 0), PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, and UNSUBACK. It is not present in CONNECT, CONNACK, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, or DISCONNECT packets.

The Remaining Length field does not include the bytes of the Fixed Header (byte 1) or the bytes used to encode the Remaining Length field. The field is encoded using a scheme where each byte contributes seven bits of data, and the most significant bit is a continuation flag (set to 1 if another byte follows). The Remaining Length field can be 1 to 4 bytes long, allowing it to represent values from 0 to 268,435,455 bytes. For example, a value of 64 is encoded as the single byte 0x40, and a value of 321 is encoded as the two-byte sequence 0xC1 0x02 (least significant byte first). The maximum value, 268,435,455, is encoded as the four-byte sequence 0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0x7F.

heading: 2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.2 Fixed header -> 2.2.2 Flags -> Table 2.2 - Flag Bits
sentence: The table shows the fixed header flags for MQTT control packets.
enhanced_sentence: Table 2.2 in the MQTT 3.1.1 specification defines the usage of the four flag bits (bits 3, 2, 1, and 0) in the fixed header for each type of MQTT control packet. For most packet types, these bits are reserved and must be set to specific values, typically 0. The exceptions are the PUBLISH, PUBREL, SUBSCRIBE, and UNSUBSCRIBE packets. For the PUBLISH packet, bit 3 is the DUP flag, bits 2 and 1 represent the QoS level, and bit 0 is the RETAIN flag. For the PUBREL, SUBSCRIBE, and UNSUBSCRIBE packets, bit 1 is set to 1 while bits 3, 2, and 0 are set to 0; all other bits for all other packet types are reserved and set to 0. The DUP flag indicates duplicate delivery, the QoS bits specify the quality of service level, and the RETAIN flag controls server message retention for the PUBLISH packet, as detailed in Section 3.3.1.

heading: 2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.2 Fixed header -> 2.2.3 Remaining Length -> Table 2.4 Size of Remaining Length field
sentence: A non-normative comment describes the algorithm for decoding the Remaining Length field: multiplier = 1; value = 0; do encodedByte = next byte from stream; value += (encodedByte AND 127) * multiplier; multiplier *= 128; if (multiplier > 128*128*128) throw Error(Malformed Remaining Length); while ((encodedByte AND 128) != 0); where AND is the bit-wise AND operator (& in C).
enhanced_sentence:
The MQTT specification defines the Remaining Length field in the fixed header of a control packet using a variable-length encoding scheme that can be 1 to 4 bytes long. The encoding represents values from 0 to 268,435,455, where each byte contributes 7 bits of data, and the most significant bit (bit 7) is a continuation flag. A value of 1 in the continuation flag indicates that the next byte is part of the Remaining Length field. The size ranges are: 1 byte for values 0 to 127, 2 bytes for 128 to 16,383, 3 bytes for 16,384 to 2,097,151, and 4 bytes for 2,097,152 to 268,435,455.

A non-normative comment provides the following algorithm for decoding the Remaining Length field from a stream of bytes:
1. Initialize a multiplier to 1 and a decoded value to 0.
2. For each encoded byte read sequentially from the stream:
   a. Add the product of (the encoded byte bitwise AND 127) and the current multiplier to the decoded value.
   b. Multiply the multiplier by 128.
   c. If the multiplier exceeds 128³ (i.e., 2,097,152), the encoding is malformed, and an error must be thrown.
3. Continue reading bytes while the most significant bit (bit 7) of the encoded byte is set to 1 (i.e., (encoded byte bitwise AND 128) is not equal to 0).
When the algorithm terminates, the decoded value variable contains the Remaining Length. The bitwise AND operation is denoted as & in the C programming language.

heading: 2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.3 Variable header -> 2.3.1 Packet Identifier -> Figure 2.3 - Packet Identifier bytes
sentence: These Control Packets are PUBLISH (where QoS > 0), PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK.
enhanced_sentence: The variable header component of many MQTT Control Packet types includes a 2-byte Packet Identifier field. Specifically, the Control Packets that contain this field are: PUBLISH (but only when its QoS level is greater than 0), PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, and UNSUBACK. Among these, the SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE, and PUBLISH (with QoS > 0) Control Packets MUST contain a non-zero 16-bit Packet Identifier. Each time a Client sends a new packet of one of these types, it MUST assign a currently unused Packet Identifier. If a Client re-sends a particular Control Packet, it MUST use the same Packet Identifier in subsequent re-sends. A Packet Identifier becomes available for reuse after the Client processes the corresponding acknowledgement packet: for a QoS 1 PUBLISH, this is a PUBACK; for QoS 2, it is a PUBCOMP; for SUBSCRIBE, it is a SUBACK; and for UNSUBSCRIBE, it is an UNSUBACK. These same rules apply to a Server when it sends a PUBLISH packet with QoS greater than 0. A PUBLISH Packet MUST NOT contain a Packet Identifier if its QoS value is 0. Acknowledgement packets (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, SUBACK, UNSUBACK) MUST contain the same Packet Identifier as was used in their corresponding request packet (the original PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE, or UNSUBSCRIBE packet).

sentence: It consists of 6 bytes: byte 1 contains the length MSB (0) with binary value 00000000, byte 2 contains the length LSB (4) with binary value 00000100, byte 3 contains 'M' with binary value 01001101, byte 4 contains 'Q' with binary value 01010001, byte 5 contains 'T' with binary value 01010100, and byte 6 contains 'T' with binary value 01010100." }, { "Original": "The string, its offset and length will not be changed by future versions of the MQTT specification.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "If the protocol name is incorrect the Server MAY disconnect the Client, or it MAY continue processing the CONNECT packet in accordance with some other specification.", "Adjusted": "If the protocol name is incorrect, the Server MAY disconnect the Client, or the Server MAY continue processing the CONNECT packet in accordance with some other specification." }, { "Original": "In the latter case, the Server MUST NOT continue to process the CONNECT packet in line with this specification [MQTT-3.1.2-1].", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "**Non normative comment** Packet inspectors, such as firewalls, could use the Protocol Name to identify MQTT traffic.", "Adjusted": "Packet inspectors, such as firewalls, could use the Protocol Name to identify MQTT traffic." } ] }
enhanced_sentence: The Protocol Name field in the CONNECT packet's variable header is a UTF-8 encoded string representing the protocol name "MQTT". This field consists of 6 bytes: the first two bytes encode the length of the string that follows, and the next four bytes are the string characters. Specifically, byte 1 contains the Most Significant Byte (MSB) of the length, which is 0 (binary 00000000). Byte 2 contains the Least Significant Byte (LSB) of the length, which is 4 (binary 00000100), indicating the subsequent string is four characters long. Byte 3 contains the character 'M' (binary 01001101), byte 4 contains 'Q' (binary 01010001), byte 5 contains 'T' (binary 01010100), and byte 6 contains 'T' (binary 01010100). The string "MQTT", its offset within the packet, and its encoded length are fixed and will not be changed by future versions of the MQTT specification. If the Server receives an incorrect protocol name, it has two options: it MAY disconnect the Client, or it MAY continue processing the CONNECT packet as defined by a different specification. If the Server chooses the latter option, it MUST NOT continue to process the CONNECT packet according to this MQTT specification [MQTT-3.1.2-1]. As a non-normative note, network devices like firewalls can inspect this Protocol Name field to identify MQTT protocol traffic.

The first two bytes specify the length of the string that follows: byte 1 is the Most Significant Byte (MSB) of the length, with a value of 0 (binary 00000000), and byte 2 is the Least Significant Byte (LSB) of the length, with a value of 4 (binary 00000100), indicating a four-character string. The subsequent four bytes contain the UTF-8 characters: byte 3 is 'M' (binary 01001101), byte 4 is 'Q' (binary 01010001), byte 5 is 'T' (binary 01010100), and byte 6 is 'T' (binary 01010100). The capitalization "MQTT" must be used as shown. If a Server receives an incorrect protocol name, it MAY disconnect the Client or MAY continue processing the CONNECT packet according to a different specification; however, if it chooses the latter, the Server MUST NOT continue processing the packet according to the MQTT specification [MQTT-3.1.2-1]. The string, its offset, and its length are fixed and will not change in future versions of the MQTT specification. Non-normatively, packet inspectors like firewalls can use this Protocol Name to identify MQTT traffic.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.2 CONNACK â Acknowledge connection request -> 3.2.2 Variable header -> 3.2.2.2 Session Present
sentence: If the Server accepts a connection with CleanSession set to 0, the value set in Session Present depends on whether the Server already has stored Session state for the supplied client ID.
enhanced_sentence: When the MQTT server accepts a connection request (CONNECT packet) where the client has set the CleanSession flag to 0, the server determines the value for the Session Present flag in the CONNACK packet's variable header based on existing session state. Specifically, if the server has previously stored a persistent session state for the client identifier supplied in the CONNECT packet, the server MUST set the Session Present flag to 1 in the CONNACK packet. If the server does not have any stored session state for that client identifier, it MUST set the Session Present flag to 0. This setting of the Session Present flag occurs in addition to the server setting a zero return code (Connection Accepted) in the CONNACK packet.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH â Publish message -> 3.3.1 Fixed header -> Figure 3.10 â PUBLISH Packet fixed header
sentence: Byte 1 contains the MQTT Control Packet type (3), DUP flag, QoS level, RETAIN flag, bits 0 and 1 set to 0, bits 2 and 3 set to 1, and bits 4 through 7 marked as X.
enhanced_sentence:
In the fixed header of an MQTT PUBLISH Control Packet, the first byte (Byte 1) is structured as follows, with bit positions numbered from 7 (most significant bit) to 0 (least significant bit):
- Bits 7-4 represent the MQTT Control Packet type. For a PUBLISH packet, this type value is 3 (binary 0011).
- Bit 3 is the DUP flag, which indicates duplicate delivery.
- Bits 2-1 are the QoS flags, which specify the Quality of Service level for the message.
- Bit 0 is the RETAIN flag.
- Bits 0 and 1 of the QoS field are used to encode the QoS level; bits 2 and 3 of the QoS field are reserved and must be set to 0 and 1, respectively, as per the specification.
- Bits 4 through 7 of the QoS field are marked as 'X' in the figure, indicating they are part of the Packet type and not the QoS field.

The second byte (Byte 2) of the fixed header contains the Remaining Length field, which encodes the number of bytes in the variable header and payload that follow.

enhanced_sentence:
The Clean Session flag is located at bit 1 of the Connect Flags byte in the CONNECT packet's variable header. If the Clean Session flag is set to 0, the Server MUST resume communications with the Client based on state from the existing Session identified by the Client identifier. If no Session exists for that Client identifier, the Server MUST create a new Session. Both the Client and Server MUST store the Session state after they disconnect. Following the disconnection of a Session with Clean Session set to 0, the Server MUST store any subsequent QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages that match the Client's subscriptions at the time of disconnection as part of the Session state; the Server MAY also store QoS 0 messages meeting the same criteria.

If the Clean Session flag is set to 1, both the Client and Server MUST discard any previous Session and start a new one. This new Session lasts only as long as the Network Connection, and its state data MUST NOT be reused in any subsequent Session.

The Session state in the Client consists of: QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages sent to the Server but not completely acknowledged, and QoS 2 messages received from the Server but not completely acknowledged. The Session state in the Server consists of: the existence of a Session; the Client's subscriptions; QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages sent to the Client but not completely acknowledged; QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages pending transmission to the Client; QoS 2 messages received from the Client but not completely acknowledged; and optionally, QoS 0 messages pending transmission to the Client. Retained messages are not part of the Server's Session state and MUST NOT be deleted when a Session ends.

When Clean Session is set to 1, the Client and Server are not required to delete the old Session state atomically. For consistent state in case of failure, a Client should repeatedly attempt to connect with Clean Session set to 1 until successful. Typically, a Client uses either Clean Session set to 0 or set to 1 consistently. A Client using Clean Session set to 1 will not receive messages published while it was disconnected and must re-subscribe each time it connects. A Client using Clean Session set to 0 will receive all QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages published during its disconnection. Therefore, to avoid losing messages while disconnected, use QoS 1 or QoS 2 with Clean Session set to 0. A Client should only use Clean Session set to 0 if it intends to reconnect later. When a Client no longer needs a session, it should perform a final connect with Clean Session set to 1 before disconnecting.

enhanced_sentence: In the MQTT protocol, when a Server forwards a PUBLISH Packet to a subscribing Client, the Topic Name contained within that PUBLISH Packet MUST match the Client's Subscription’s Topic Filter. This matching is performed using the rules defined in Section 4.7 of the MQTT v3.1.1 specification, which governs how Topic Filters, which can contain wildcard characters, match against Topic Names, which must not contain wildcards. This requirement ensures the Client receives publications only for the topics to which it subscribed. It is noted that the Server is permitted to modify the Topic Name before forwarding, so the matching Topic Name in the forwarded packet may differ from the Topic Name in the original publication received by the Server.

enhanced_sentence: The Client Identifier (ClientId) identifies the Client to the Server. Each Client connecting to the Server has a unique ClientId. The ClientId MUST be used by Clients and by Servers to identify state that the Clients and Servers hold relating to the MQTT Session between that Client and the Server [MQTT-3.1.3-2]. The Client Identifier (ClientId) MUST be present and MUST be the first field in the CONNECT packet payload [MQTT-3.1.3-3]. The ClientId MUST be a UTF-8 encoded string as defined in Section 1.5.3 [MQTT-3.1.3-4]. The Server MUST allow ClientIds which are between 1 and 23 UTF-8 encoded bytes in length, and that contain only the characters "0123456789abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ" [MQTT-3.1.3-5]. The Server MAY allow ClientIds that contain more than 23 encoded bytes. The Server MAY allow ClientIds that contain characters not included in the list given above. A Server MAY allow a Client to supply a ClientId that has a length of zero bytes; however, if the Server does so, the Server MUST treat this as a special case and assign a unique ClientId to that Client. The Server MUST then process the CONNECT packet as if the Client had provided that unique ClientId [MQTT-3.1.3-6]. If the Client supplies a zero-byte ClientId, the Client MUST also set the CleanSession flag in the CONNECT packet to 1 [MQTT-3.1.3-7]. If the Client supplies a zero-byte ClientId with the CleanSession flag set to 0, the Server MUST respond to the CONNECT Packet with a CONNACK return code 0x02 (Identifier rejected) and then close the Network Connection [MQTT-3.1.3-8]. If the Server rejects the ClientId for any reason, the Server MUST respond to the CONNECT Packet with a CONNACK return code 0x02 (Identifier rejected) and then close the Network Connection [MQTT-3.1.3-9].

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH â Publish message -> 3.3.1 Fixed header -> 3.3.1.1 DUP
sentence: The DUP flag in the outgoing PUBLISH packet is set independently of the incoming PUBLISH packet; its value MUST be determined solely by whether the outgoing PUBLISH packet is a retransmission [MQTT-3.3.1-3].
enhanced_sentence: When a Server forwards a PUBLISH packet to subscribers, the Server sets the DUP flag in the outgoing PUBLISH packet based only on whether that specific outgoing transmission is a retransmission by the Server. The value of the DUP flag from the original incoming PUBLISH packet is not used or copied; the outgoing DUP flag MUST be 1 only if the outgoing packet is a retransmission, and 0 otherwise [MQTT-3.3.1-3].

In the first byte (byte 1), bits 7 through 0 represent the MQTT Control Packet type and the reserved flags. sentence: { "3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe to topics": [ { "Original": "The SUBSCRIBE Packet is sent from the Client to the Server to create one or more Subscriptions.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "Each Subscription registers a Clientâ\x80\x99s interest in one or more Topics.", "Adjusted": "Each Subscription registers a Client's interest in one or more Topics." }, { "Original": "The Server sends PUBLISH Packets to the Client in order to forward Application Messages that were published to Topics that match these Subscriptions.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "The SUBSCRIBE Packet also specifies (for each Subscription) the maximum QoS with which the Server can send Application Messages to the Client.", "Adjusted": "No change" } ] }
enhanced_sentence: The SUBSCRIBE control packet is sent from an MQTT Client to an MQTT Server to create one or more Subscriptions. Each Subscription registers that specific Client's interest in one or more Topics. Consequently, the Server will send PUBLISH packets to that Client to forward any Application Messages that were published to Topics matching these Subscriptions. Furthermore, within the SUBSCRIBE packet, the Client specifies, for each Subscription, the maximum Quality of Service (QoS) level that the Server is permitted to use when sending those matching Application Messages to the Client.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe to topics -> 3.8.1 Fixed header -> Figure 3.20 â SUBSCRIBE Packet fixed header
sentence: Byte 1 contains the MQTT Control Packet type (8) and reserved bits set to 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0.
enhanced_sentence: In the MQTT SUBSCRIBE packet, the first byte of the fixed header (Byte 1) specifies the packet type and contains reserved bits. The MQTT Control Packet type for SUBSCRIBE is the binary value 8, which occupies bits 7-4 of this byte. The remaining bits 3-0 are reserved and must be set to specific values: bit 3 must be 1, bit 2 must be 0, bit 1 must be 0, and bit 0 must be 0. Therefore, the complete binary pattern for Byte 1 is 1000 (for the packet type) followed by 1, 0, 0, 0 (for the reserved bits), resulting in the binary value 10001000, which corresponds to the decimal value 136. Any other value for these reserved bits is malformed, and the server must close the network connection.

heading: 3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE - Subscribe to topics -> 3.8.3 Payload -> Figure 3.22 â SUBSCRIBE Packet payload format
sentence: The SUBSCRIBE packet payload format includes: Description columns for bits 7 through 0, Topic Filter byte 1, Length MSB byte 2, Length LSB bytes 3 through N, Topic Filter Requested QoS, Reserved, and QoS byte N+1 with bits set to 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, X, X.
enhanced_sentence:
The payload of a SUBSCRIBE packet contains one or more Topic Filter entries. Each entry consists of two consecutive fields: a Topic Filter field followed by a Requested QoS byte field.

The **Topic Filter field** is a UTF-8 encoded string. It is structured with a 2-byte length prefix followed by the string data. Specifically, byte 1 of this field is the Most Significant Byte (MSB) of the string length, byte 2 is the Least Significant Byte (LSB) of the length, and bytes 3 through N contain the string characters.

Immediately following the Topic Filter field is the **Requested QoS byte field** (byte N+1 of the entry). This single byte specifies the maximum Quality of Service level for the subscription. Only the two least significant bits (bits 1 and 0) of this byte are used to encode the QoS value (0, 1, or 2). The six most significant bits (bits 7 through 2) are reserved and must be set to zero. In the specification's bit notation, this is represented as bits set to `0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, X, X`, where 'X' denotes the two QoS bits.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows
sentence: MQTT delivers Application Messages according to the Quality of Service (QoS) levels defined here.
enhanced_sentence: The MQTT protocol delivers Application Messages between a single Sender and a single Receiver according to the defined Quality of Service (QoS) levels: QoS 0 (at most once), QoS 1 (at least once), and QoS 2 (exactly once). This delivery mechanism is symmetric, meaning both the Client and the Server can act as either the Sender or the Receiver. When the Server delivers an Application Message to multiple Clients, it treats each Client independently, and the outbound QoS level to a Client may differ from the inbound QoS level at which the Server received the message.

enhanced_sentence: In the MQTT Quality of Service level 0 (QoS 0) delivery protocol, the Receiver (typically the MQTT client or server acting as a subscriber) accepts ownership of the application message at the moment the Receiver successfully receives the PUBLISH control packet from the Sender. This acceptance occurs without the Receiver sending any acknowledgment packet back to the Sender. Consequently, message delivery relies on the underlying network's capabilities, with no retransmission by the Sender, meaning the message is delivered at most once.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> _4.3.2_ _QoS 1: At least once delivery_ -> Figure 4.2 â QoS 1 protocol flow diagram, non normative example
sentence: The receiver initiates onward delivery of the application message and sends a PUBACK packet with the packet identifier.
enhanced_sentence: Upon receiving a PUBLISH packet with QoS level 1, the receiver (typically a server) initiates onward delivery of the application message to its final destination and sends a PUBACK packet containing the same Packet Identifier as the received PUBLISH packet. This PUBACK is sent without waiting for the onward delivery to complete, thereby transferring responsibility for the message to the receiver.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> _4.3.2_ _QoS 1: At least once delivery_ -> Figure 4.2 â QoS 1 protocol flow diagram, non normative example
sentence: The sender stores the message and sends a PUBLISH packet with QoS 1 and DUP 0.
enhanced_sentence: In the MQTT protocol, when a client or server (the sender) transmits an application message with Quality of Service (QoS) level 1, the sender first stores a local copy of that message. The sender then transmits a PUBLISH control packet containing the message. This PUBLISH packet has its QoS field set to 1 (indicating at least once delivery) and its DUP flag set to 0 (indicating this is the first transmission attempt for this packet identifier).

enhanced_sentence:
Figure 3.23 provides a non-normative example of the SUBSCRIBE packet payload format, illustrating the encoding of two topic filters and their requested QoS levels. The payload is structured as a series of topic filter entries, each consisting of a UTF-8 encoded string preceded by its two-byte length and followed by a single-byte QoS value.

The first topic filter is the string "a/b". Its encoding begins with two length bytes: byte 1 is the Most Significant Byte (MSB) of the length, with a value of 0 (bits 00000000), and byte 2 is the Least Significant Byte (LSB) of the length, with a value of 3 (bits 00000011), indicating the string is three bytes long. The subsequent three bytes contain the UTF-8 characters: byte 3 is 'a' (0x61, bits 01100001), byte 4 is '/' (0x2F, bits 00101111), and byte 5 is 'b' (0x62, bits 01100010). This topic filter entry concludes with byte 6, the Requested QoS, which has a value of 1 (bits 00000001).

Immediately following is the second topic filter, "c/d". Its encoding is identical in structure: byte 7 is the length MSB (0, bits 00000000), byte 8 is the length LSB (3, bits 00000011), byte 9 is 'c' (0x63, bits 01100011), byte 10 is '/' (0x2F, bits 00101111), and byte 11 is 'd' (0x64, bits 01100100). The entry ends with byte 12, the Requested QoS, which has a value of 2 (bits 00000010).

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.4 Message delivery retry
sentence: When a Client reconnects with CleanSession set to 0, both the Client and Server MUST re-send any unacknowledged PUBLISH Packets (where QoS > 0) and PUBREL Packets using their original Packet Identifiers [MQTT-4.4.0-1].
enhanced_sentence: When an MQTT Client reconnects to an MQTT Server with the CleanSession flag in the CONNECT packet set to 0, both the Client and the Server MUST re-send any previously sent but unacknowledged control packets. Specifically, this requirement applies to PUBLISH packets with a Quality of Service (QoS) level greater than 0 and to PUBREL packets. These packets MUST be re-sent using their original Packet Identifiers [MQTT-4.4.0-1]. This scenario—reconnecting with CleanSession set to 0—is the only circumstance in which a Client or Server is REQUIRED to redeliver messages.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.5 Message receipt
sentence: The Client MUST acknowledge any Publish Packet the Client receives according to the applicable QoS rules regardless of whether the Client elects to process the Application Message that the Publish Packet contains [MQTT-4.5.0-2].
enhanced_sentence: The MQTT Client MUST acknowledge any Publish Packet it receives by sending the appropriate response packet (a PUBACK for QoS 1, or PUBREC for QoS 2) as defined by the QoS level of that Publish Packet. This acknowledgment obligation exists regardless of whether the Client chooses to process or handle the Application Message payload contained within the Publish Packet.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.5 Message receipt
sentence: When a Server takes ownership of an incoming Application Message, the Server MUST add the Application Message to the Session state of those clients that have matching Subscriptions.
enhanced_sentence: When an MQTT Server receives an Application Message in a PUBLISH packet and takes ownership of that message (as defined by the protocol), the Server MUST add the Application Message to the Session state of each Client that has a current Subscription whose Topic Filter matches the message's Topic Name. The rules for determining a match between a Topic Name and a Topic Filter are defined in Section 4.7 of the MQTT specification.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards
sentence: { "4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards": [ { "Original": "The topic level separator is used to introduce structure into the Topic Name.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "If present, it divides the Topic Name into multiple â\x80\x9ctopic levelsâ\x80\x9d.", "Adjusted": "If present, the topic level separator divides the Topic Name into multiple topic levels." }, { "Original": "A subscriptionâ\x80\x99s Topic Filter can contain special wildcard characters, which allow you to subscribe to multiple topics at once.", "Adjusted": "A subscription's Topic Filter can contain special wildcard characters, which allow you to subscribe to multiple topics at once." }, { "Original": "The wildcard characters can be used in Topic Filters, but MUST NOT be used within a Topic Name [MQTT-4.7.1-1].", "Adjusted": "No change" } ] }
enhanced_sentence: The topic level separator (the forward slash character '/') introduces a hierarchical structure into the MQTT Topic Name. When present, the topic level separator divides the Topic Name into multiple discrete segments called "topic levels." A client's subscription Topic Filter can contain the special wildcard characters, the single-level wildcard '+' and the multi-level wildcard '#', which allow a single subscription to match multiple Topic Names. These wildcard characters are permitted only within Topic Filters and are explicitly prohibited from being used within a Topic Name itself [MQTT-4.7.1-1].

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.1 Topic level separator
sentence: { "4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.1 Topic level separator": [ { "Original": "The forward slash (â\x80\x98/â\x80\x99 U+002F) is used to separate each level within a topic tree and provide a hierarchical structure to the Topic Names.", "Adjusted": "The forward slash ('/' U+002F) is used to separate each level within a topic tree and provide a hierarchical structure to the Topic Names." }, { "Original": "The use of the topic level separator is significant when either of the two wildcard characters is encountered in Topic Filters specified by subscribing Clients.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "Topic level separators can appear anywhere in a Topic Filter or Topic Name.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "Adjacent Topic level separators indicate a zero length topic level.", "Adjusted": "No change" } ] }
enhanced_sentence: The forward slash character '/' (Unicode U+002F) serves as the topic level separator in MQTT. This separator divides a topic name or topic filter into distinct levels, creating a hierarchical topic tree structure. The presence of the topic level separator is particularly important when a subscribing client's topic filter includes either of the two wildcard characters (the single-level '+' or the multi-level '#'), as the separator defines the levels that these wildcards match. The topic level separator can be placed at any position within a topic name or topic filter. When two topic level separators appear consecutively, they denote a topic level with a length of zero characters.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of Service levels and protocol flows -> 4.3.3 QoS 2: Exactly once delivery -> Figure 4.3 â QoS 2 protocol flow diagram, non normative example
sentence: The protocol flow diagram shows: Sender Action: Store message; Control Packet: PUBLISH QoS 2, DUP 0; Receiver Action: Method A, Store message.
enhanced_sentence: The protocol flow diagram for QoS 2 (Figure 4.3) illustrates the sequence of control packets and actions. In the depicted non-normative example, the sender's initial action is to store the application message. The sender then transmits a PUBLISH control packet with QoS level 2 and the DUP flag set to 0. Upon receiving this PUBLISH packet, the receiver (using Method A) also stores the application message. The flow continues with the receiver sending a PUBREC packet containing the packet identifier. The sender, upon receiving the PUBREC, discards its stored application message and stores the state of the received PUBREC. The sender then sends a PUBREL packet with the packet identifier. The receiver, upon receiving the PUBREL, initiates onward delivery of the application message to the subscribing application and then discards its stored message. Finally, the sender sends a PUBCOMP packet with the packet identifier, and upon receiving it, the receiver discards all stored state related to this packet identifier. The diagram notes that the receiver may choose between Method A and Method B, which differ in the point at which the message is made available for onward delivery; this choice is implementation-specific and does not affect the QoS 2 guarantee of exactly-once delivery.

enhanced_sentence:
The Quality of Service level 1 (QoS 1) protocol ensures that an Application Message is delivered to the Receiver at least once. A PUBLISH packet sent with QoS=1 contains a Packet Identifier in its variable header. The protocol flow involves specific requirements for both the Sender and the Receiver of the message.

For the Sender:
1.  Each time the Sender has a new Application Message to publish, the Sender MUST assign a currently unused Packet Identifier.
2.  The Sender MUST then send a PUBLISH packet containing this Packet Identifier with the QoS field set to 1 and the DUP flag set to 0.
3.  The Sender MUST treat that PUBLISH packet as "unacknowledged" (see discussion in Section 4.4) until the Sender receives the corresponding PUBACK packet from the Receiver.
4.  The Packet Identifier becomes available for the Sender to reuse only after the Sender has received the PUBACK packet. While waiting for acknowledgements, the Sender is permitted to send further PUBLISH packets with different Packet Identifiers.

For the Receiver:
1.  Upon receiving a QoS 1 PUBLISH packet and accepting ownership of its Application Message, the Receiver MUST respond with a PUBACK packet containing the Packet Identifier from the received PUBLISH packet.
2.  After the Receiver has sent that PUBACK packet, if the Receiver receives any subsequent PUBLISH packet containing the same Packet Identifier, the Receiver MUST treat it as a new publication, regardless of the setting of its DUP flag.

This interaction is defined by the normative statements [MQTT-4.3.2-1] and [MQTT-4.3.2-2]. Further details on Packet Identifiers are provided in Section 2.3.1.

sentence: { "4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.1 Topic wildcards -> 4.7.1.2 Multi-level wildcard": [ { "Original": "The number sign (â\x80\x98#â\x80\x99 U+0023) is a wildcard character that matches any number of levels within a topic.", "Adjusted": "The number sign ('#' U+0023) is a wildcard character that matches any number of levels within a topic." }, { "Original": "The multi-level wildcard represents the parent and any number of child levels.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "The multi-level wildcard character MUST be specified either on its own or following a topic level separator.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "In either case it MUST be the last character specified in the Topic Filter [MQTT-4.7.1-2].", "Adjusted": "In either case the multi-level wildcard character MUST be the last character specified in the Topic Filter [MQTT-4.7.1-2]." }, { "Original": "**Non normative comment** For example, if a Client subscribes to â\x80\x9csport/tennis/player1/#â\x80\x9d, it would receive messages published using these topic names: · â\x80\x9csport/tennis/player1â\x80\x9d · â\x80\x9csport/tennis/player1/rankingâ\x80\x9d · â\x80\x9csport/tennis/player1/score/wimbledonâ\x80\x9d **Non normative comment** · â\x80\x9csport/#â\x80\x9d also matches the singular â\x80\x9csportâ\x80\x9d, since # includes the parent level.", "Adjusted": "For example, if a Client subscribes to 'sport/tennis/player1/#', the Client would receive messages published using these topic names: 'sport/tennis/player1', 'sport/tennis/player1/ranking', and 'sport/tennis/player1/score/wimbledon'.
enhanced_sentence:
The multi-level wildcard character is the number sign ('#' U+0023). When used in a Topic Filter, the multi-level wildcard matches any number of levels within a topic name, including zero levels. This means the wildcard represents the parent level where it is placed and any number of child levels beneath it. The multi-level wildcard character MUST be specified either on its own (e.g., '#') or following a topic level separator (e.g., 'sport/tennis/#'). In either case, the multi-level wildcard character MUST be the last character specified in the Topic Filter [MQTT-4.7.1-2]; no characters are permitted after it within that filter.

For example, if a Client subscribes to the Topic Filter 'sport/tennis/player1/#', the Client would receive messages published to the topic names 'sport/tennis/player1', 'sport/tennis/player1/ranking', and 'sport/tennis/player1/score/wimbledon'. Similarly, the Topic Filter 'sport/#' matches the singular topic name 'sport' because the multi-level wildcard includes the parent level. Valid uses include '#' (which matches all topic names) and 'sport/tennis/#'. Invalid uses include 'sport/tennis#' (missing the level separator before '#') and 'sport/tennis/#/ranking' (because characters appear after the multi-level wildcard).

"Adjusted": "**Non normative comment** \"+\" is valid; \"+/tennis/#\" is valid; \"sport+\" is not valid; \"sport/+/player1\" is valid; \"/finance\" matches \"+/+\" and \"/+\", but not \"+\"." } ] }
enhanced_sentence:
The single-level wildcard character is the plus sign ('+' U+002B). When used in a Topic Filter, the single-level wildcard matches exactly one topic level. The single-level wildcard can appear at any position within a Topic Filter, including the first and last levels, and it can appear at multiple levels within the same filter. The single-level wildcard can also be used in combination with the multilevel wildcard ('#'). Wherever the single-level wildcard is used, it MUST occupy an entire level of the Topic Filter; it cannot be combined with other characters within the same level [MQTT-4.7.1-3].

**Non-normative examples:**
*   The Topic Filter `"sport/tennis/+"` matches Topic Names like `"sport/tennis/player1"` and `"sport/tennis/player2"`, but does not match `"sport/tennis/player1/ranking"` because the wildcard matches only one level.
*   Because it matches only a single level, the Topic Filter `"sport/+"` does not match the Topic Name `"sport"`, but it does match `"sport/"`.
*   Valid Topic Filters include: `"+"`; `"+/tennis/#"`; `"sport/+/player1"`.
*   An invalid Topic Filter is `"sport+"`, as the wildcard does not occupy an entire level.
*   The Topic Name `"/finance"` matches the Topic Filters `"+/+"` and `"/+"`, but does not match `"+"`.

enhanced_sentence:
When an MQTT Server receives a SUBSCRIBE Packet from a Client, the Server MUST respond with a SUBACK Packet [MQTT-3.8.4-1]. The SUBACK Packet MUST have the same Packet Identifier as the SUBSCRIBE Packet that the SUBACK Packet is acknowledging [MQTT-3.8.4-2]. The Server is permitted to start sending PUBLISH packets matching the new Subscription before the Server sends the SUBACK Packet.

If a Server receives a SUBSCRIBE Packet containing a Topic Filter that is identical to an existing Subscription's Topic Filter, then the Server MUST completely replace that existing Subscription with a new Subscription. The Topic Filter in the new Subscription will be identical to the Topic Filter in the previous Subscription, although the new Subscription's maximum QoS value could be different. Any existing retained messages matching the Topic Filter MUST be re-sent to the Client, but the ongoing flow of non-retained PUBLISH packets MUST NOT be interrupted [MQTT-3.8.4-3]. Where the Topic Filter is not identical to any existing Subscription's filter, a new Subscription is created and all matching retained messages are sent.

If a Server receives a SUBSCRIBE packet that contains multiple Topic Filters, the Server MUST handle that packet as if it had received a sequence of multiple SUBSCRIBE packets, except that the Server combines their responses into a single SUBACK response [MQTT-3.8.4-4]. The SUBACK Packet sent by the Server to the Client MUST contain a return code for each Topic Filter/QoS pair. This return code MUST either show the maximum QoS that was granted for that Subscription or indicate that the subscription failed [MQTT-3.8.4-5]. The Server might grant a lower maximum QoS than the subscriber requested.

The QoS of Payload Messages (PUBLISH packets) sent in response to a Subscription MUST be the minimum of the QoS of the originally published message and the maximum QoS granted by the Server for that Subscription. The server is permitted to send duplicate copies of a message to a subscriber in the case where the original message was published with QoS 1 and the maximum QoS granted was QoS 0 [MQTT-3.8.4-6].

**Non-normative examples:** If a subscribing Client has been granted a maximum QoS of 1 for a particular Topic Filter, then a QoS 0 Application Message matching the filter is delivered to the Client at QoS 0. This means that at most one copy of the message is received by the Client. On the other hand, a QoS 2 Message published to the same topic is downgraded by the Server to QoS 1 for delivery to the Client, so that Client might receive duplicate copies of the Message. If the subscribing Client has been granted a maximum QoS of 0, then an Application Message originally published as QoS 2 might get lost on the hop to the Client, but the Server should never send a duplicate of that Message. A QoS 1 Message published to the same topic might either get lost or duplicated on its transmission to that Client.

**Non-normative comment:** Subscribing to a Topic Filter at QoS 2 is equivalent to saying "I would like to receive Messages matching this filter at the QoS with which they were published". This means a publisher is responsible for determining the maximum QoS a Message can be delivered at, but a subscriber is able to require that the Server downgrades the QoS to one more suitable for its usage.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.2 Topics beginning with
sentence: { "4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.2 Topics beginning with": [ { "Original": "The Server MUST NOT match Topic Filters starting with a wildcard character (# or +) with Topic Names beginning with a character [MQTT-4.7.2-1].", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "The Server SHOULD prevent Clients from using such Topic Names to exchange messages with other Clients.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "Server implementations MAY use Topic Names that start with a leading character for other purposes.", "Adjusted": "No change" }, { "Original": "**Non normative comment** · SYS/ has been widely adopted as a prefix to topics that contain Server-specific information or control APIs · Applications cannot use a topic with a leading character for their own purposes **Non normative comment** · A subscription to â\x80\x9c#â\x80\x9d will not receive any messages published to a topic beginning with a · A subscription to â\x80\x9c+/monitor/Clientsâ\x80\x9d will not receive any messages published to â\x80\x9cSYS/monitor/Clientsâ\x80\x9d · A subscription to â\x80\x9cSYS/#â\x80\x9d will receive messages published to topics beginning with â\x80\x9cSYS/â\x80\x9d · A subscription to â\x80\x9cSYS/monitor/+â\x80\x9d will receive messages published to â\x80\x9cSYS/monitor/Clientsâ\x80\x9d · For a Client to receive messages from topics that begin with SYS/ and from topics that donâ\x80\x99t begin with a , it has to subscribe to both â\x80\x9c#â\x80\x9d and â\x80\x9cSYS/#â\x80\x9d", "Adjusted": "Non-normative comment: SYS/ has been widely adopted as a prefix to topics that contain Server-specific information or control APIs.
enhanced_sentence:
The MQTT Server MUST NOT match any Topic Filter that begins with a wildcard character (either the multi-level wildcard '#' or the single-level wildcard '+') with any Topic Name that begins with a leading character (specifically, the character '$'). [MQTT-4.7.2-1]. The Server SHOULD prevent Clients from publishing to or subscribing to such Topic Names (those beginning with '$') for the purpose of exchanging messages with other Clients. Server implementations MAY reserve Topic Names that start with the leading character '$' for their own purposes, such as for Server-specific control or information topics.

*Non-normative comment:* The prefix `$SYS/` has been widely adopted for topics containing Server-specific information or control APIs. Consequently, Client applications cannot reliably use a topic with a leading '$' character for their own purposes. For example:
*   A subscription to "#" will not receive messages published to a topic beginning with '$' (e.g., `$SYS/server/uptime`).
*   A subscription to "+/monitor/Clients" will not receive messages published to `$SYS/monitor/Clients`.
*   A subscription to `$SYS/#` *will* receive messages published to topics beginning with `$SYS/`.
*   A subscription to `$SYS/monitor/+` will receive messages published to `$SYS/monitor/Clients`.
*   For a Client to receive messages from *all* topics—both those that begin with `$SYS/` and those that do not begin with '$'—it must subscribe to both "#" and `$SYS/#`.

heading: 4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.3 Topic semantic and usage
sentence: { "4 Operational behavior -> 4.7 Topic Names and Topic Filters -> 4.7.3 Topic semantic and usage": [ { "Original": "The following rules apply to Topic Names and Topic Filters: * All Topic Names and Topic Filters MUST be at least one character long [MQTT-4.7.3-1] * Topic Names and Topic Filters are case sensitive * Topic Names and Topic Filters can include the space character * A leading or trailing â\x80\x98/â\x80\x99 creates a distinct Topic Name or Topic Filter * A Topic Name or Topic Filter consisting only of the â\x80\x98/â\x80\x99 character is valid * Topic Names and Topic Filters MUST NOT include the null character (Unicode U+0000) [Unicode] [MQTT-4.7.3-2] * Topic Names and Topic Filters are UTF-8 encoded strings, they MUST NOT encode to more than 65535 bytes [MQTT-4.7.3-3].", "Adjusted": "The following rules apply to Topic Names and Topic Filters: All Topic Names and Topic Filters MUST be at least one character long [MQTT-4.7.3-1].
enhanced_sentence:
The following rules apply to MQTT Topic Names and Topic Filters:
1.  A Topic Name or Topic Filter MUST be at least one character long [MQTT-4.7.3-1].
2.  Topic Names and Topic Filters are case-sensitive.
3.  Topic Names and Topic Filters can include the space character.
4.  A leading or trailing forward-slash character ('/') creates a distinct Topic Name or Topic Filter. For example, the Topic Name "/finance" is different from "finance".
5.  A Topic Name or Topic Filter consisting only of the forward-slash character ('/') is valid.
6.  Topic Names and Topic Filters MUST NOT include the null character (Unicode U+0000) [MQTT-4.7.3-2].
7.  Topic Names and Topic Filters are UTF-8 encoded strings, and the UTF-8 encoding of a Topic Name or Topic Filter MUST NOT be longer than 65535 bytes [MQTT-4.7.3-3].
8.  There is no limit to the number of levels in a Topic Name or Topic Filter, other than the limit imposed by the maximum UTF-8 encoded length of 65535 bytes.
9.  When performing subscription matching, the Server MUST NOT normalize Topic Names or Topic Filters, or modify or substitute any unrecognized characters [MQTT-4.7.3-4]. Each non-wildcard level in a Topic Filter must match the corresponding level in the Topic Name character-for-character for the match to succeed.

enhanced_sentence:
Quality of Service (QoS) level 2 is the highest quality of service, intended for use when neither the loss nor the duplication of Application Messages is acceptable. This guarantee comes with increased protocol overhead compared to lower QoS levels.

A PUBLISH packet sent at QoS 2 contains a Packet Identifier in its variable header, which is used to track the message through the two-step acknowledgment process. The Sender and Receiver have specific, complementary roles in this protocol flow.

**Sender Responsibilities:**
1.  When the Sender has a new Application Message to publish, the Sender must assign an unused Packet Identifier.
2.  The Sender must send a PUBLISH packet containing this Packet Identifier with the QoS field set to 2 and the DUP flag set to 0.
3.  The Sender must treat this initial PUBLISH packet as "unacknowledged" until the Sender receives the corresponding PUBREC packet from the Receiver.
4.  Upon receiving a PUBREC packet from the Receiver, the Sender must send a PUBREL packet. This PUBREL packet must contain the same Packet Identifier as the original PUBLISH packet.
5.  The Sender must then treat this PUBREL packet as "unacknowledged" until the Sender receives the corresponding PUBCOMP packet from the Receiver.
6.  The Sender must not re-send the PUBLISH packet once the Sender has sent the corresponding PUBREL packet.

The Packet Identifier becomes available for the Sender to reuse only after the Sender has received the PUBCOMP packet. The Sender is permitted to send further PUBLISH packets with different Packet Identifiers while waiting for acknowledgments for previous messages.

**Receiver Responsibilities:**
1.  Upon receiving and accepting ownership of a QoS 2 PUBLISH packet, the Receiver must respond by sending a PUBREC packet containing the Packet Identifier from the incoming PUBLISH packet.
2.  Until the Receiver has received the corresponding PUBREL packet for a given Packet Identifier, the Receiver must acknowledge any subsequent incoming PUBLISH packet with that same Packet Identifier by sending another PUBREC. The Receiver must ensure this does not cause duplicate messages to be delivered to any onward recipients.
3.  The Receiver must respond to a PUBREL packet by sending a PUBCOMP packet containing the same Packet Identifier as the PUBREL.
4.  After the Receiver has sent a PUBCOMP packet for a given Packet Identifier, the Receiver must treat any subsequent incoming PUBLISH packet containing that Packet Identifier as an entirely new publication.

enhanced_sentence:
A Client MUST follow these rules when implementing the protocol flows defined elsewhere in this chapter:
* When the Client re-sends any PUBLISH packets, the Client MUST re-send them in the order in which the original PUBLISH packets were sent (this applies to QoS 1 and QoS 2 messages) [MQTT-4.6.0-1].
* The Client MUST send PUBACK packets in the order in which the corresponding PUBLISH packets were received (for QoS 1 messages) [MQTT-4.6.0-2].
* The Client MUST send PUBREC packets in the order in which the corresponding PUBLISH packets were received (for QoS 2 messages) [MQTT-4.6.0-3].
* The Client MUST send PUBREL packets in the order in which the corresponding PUBREC packets were received (for QoS 2 messages) [MQTT-4.6.0-4].

A Server MUST by default treat each Topic as an "Ordered Topic". The Server MAY provide an administrative or other mechanism to allow one or more Topics to be treated as an "Unordered Topic" [MQTT-4.6.0-5].

When a Server processes a message that has been published to an Ordered Topic, the Server MUST follow the rules listed above when delivering messages to each of its subscribers. In addition, the Server MUST send PUBLISH packets to consumers (for the same Topic and QoS) in the order that the PUBLISH packets were received from any given Client [MQTT-4.6.0-6].

**Non-normative comment:** The rules listed above ensure that when a stream of messages is published and subscribed to with QoS 1, the final copy of each message received by the subscribers will be in the order that the messages were originally published in. However, the possibility of message duplication could result in a re-send of an earlier message being received after one of its successor messages. For example, a publisher might send messages in the order 1,2,3,4 and the subscriber might receive them in the order 1,2,3,2,3,4. If both the publishing Client and the Server make sure that no more than one message is "in-flight" at any one time (by not sending a message until its predecessor has been acknowledged), then no QoS 1 message will be received after any later one—for example, a subscriber might receive them in the order 1,2,3,3,4 but not 1,2,3,2,3,4. Setting an in-flight window of 1 also means that order will be preserved even if the publisher sends a sequence of messages with different QoS levels on the same topic.
